plainMeaning: This is the third vow — not to take what is not given — where I promise never to take valuable or forbidden things that aren't mine, allow myself only the smallest unavoidable items, and now examine the five ways I may have slipped.
recitation: 3. trījuṁ sthūla adattādāna-viramaṇavrata. sacitta, acitta rājanigraha-kārīuṁ, piyāruṁ aṇadīdhuṁ levā niyama. sūkṣma tṛṇā, iṁdhaṇā, pathipatita vavahāra-niyoge, dāṇacorī-jayaṇā. e trījā sthūla adattādāna-viramaṇavratataṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. tenāhaḍe, takkarappaoge, viruddharajjāikamme, kūḍatullakūḍamāṇe, tappaḍiruvagavavahāre. e pāṁca atiyāra māṁhe.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal numeral marking the third aṇuvrata.
wordByWord.notes: Numbers the third of the twelve vows; not a page-marker.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sthūla, ‘gross, large’; opposed to sūkṣma.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the householder’s limited (aṇu) form of the vow versus the ascetic’s total abstinence.
Defined in preface glossary; used here as the vow's object.
The two classes of stealable property: living and non-living.

wordByWord.gloss: royal seizure/punishment (i.e. contraband, things whose taking the king punishes)
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies goods whose theft incurs state penalty — smuggled or forbidden property.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati participial < Sanskrit kāri, ‘doing/causing’, root kṛ.
wordByWord.notes: ‘rājanigraha-kārīuṁ’ = ‘that which provokes royal penalty’.
Specifies the prized objects covered by the firm rule.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati aṇa- (negative) + dīdhuṁ (given), past participle of devuṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Vernacular restatement of a-datta; the very thing the vow forbids.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati infinitive of levuṁ < Sanskrit labh, ‘to obtain’.

wordByWord.gloss: firm rule (here: not to take precious/contraband un-given goods)
wordByWord.notes: Preface-glossary term; here it binds the householder against taking valuable un-given property.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sūkṣma, ‘fine, minute’; opposed to sthūla above.
wordByWord.notes: Introduces the trivial items the leeway (jayaṇā) tolerates.
Example of a value-less item taken without explicit permission.

wordByWord.etymology: pathi (on the road) + patita (fallen), from pat, ‘to fall’.
wordByWord.notes: Discarded or dropped roadside objects — picked up without it counting as theft.

wordByWord.gloss: in the course of ordinary dealing/customary use
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vavahāra < Sanskrit vyavahāra (transaction) + niyoga (application, engagement).
wordByWord.notes: Frames the leeway as confined to normal, good-faith everyday transactions.

wordByWord.etymology: dāṇa (toll, customs duty) + corī (theft) + jayaṇā/yatanā (prudent leeway).
wordByWord.notes: jayaṇā is a preface-glossary term; here it concedes only careful minimum in unavoidable toll matters, not deliberate evasion.
Repeated in the closing formula naming the vow.

wordByWord.gloss: belonging to the vow of abstaining

wordByWord.etymology: Book's spelling (with ય) of atichāra; Sanskrit ati-cāra, ‘over-stepping’.
wordByWord.notes: Preface-glossary term; retained verbatim per the book's orthography.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati śodhvuṁ < Sanskrit śodh / śudh, ‘to cleanse, search out’.
wordByWord.notes: The act of self-examination that the whole confession enacts.
First atichāra = stenāhṛta: knowingly dealing in goods a thief has taken.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit takkara < Sanskrit taskara (thief) + prayoga (employment, instigation).
wordByWord.notes: Second atichāra = taskara-prayoga: abetting or commissioning theft.

wordByWord.gloss: acting against state law / dealings in a hostile realm
wordByWord.etymology: viruddha (contrary) + rajja < Sanskrit rājya (state) + atikrama (transgression) + kamma/karma (act).
wordByWord.notes: Third atichāra = viruddha-rājyātikrama: smuggling or breaching the lawful order of the state.

wordByWord.etymology: kūḍa < Sanskrit kūṭa (false) + tullā/tulā (scales, weight) + kūḍa-māṇa < kūṭa-māna (false measure).
wordByWord.notes: Fourth atichāra = kūṭa-tulā-kūṭa-māna: cheating by rigged scales and measures — taking more when selling, giving less when buying.

wordByWord.etymology: tat (that) + paḍiruvaga < Sanskrit pratirūpaka (imitation, counterfeit) + vavahāra < vyavahāra (transaction).
wordByWord.notes: Fifth atichāra = tat-pratirūpaka-vyavahāra: passing off adulterated or imitation goods as genuine.

wordByWord.gloss: among, within (whichever of these I incurred)
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati < Prakrit majjha / Sanskrit madhye, ‘in the midst of’.
wordByWord.notes: Elliptical close: ‘among these five [whatever lapse occurred …]’, completed by the recurring day/night confession-refrain.

literalTranslation: Third: the gross vow of abstaining from taking-what-is-not-given. The rule — not to take, un-given, animate or inanimate things that are precious or that incur royal penalty; with prudent leeway, in the course of ordinary dealing, for trifling things — grass, firewood, what has fallen on the path — and for toll/customs matters. I scrutinise the five transgressions of this third gross vow of abstaining from taking-what-is-not-given: receiving stolen goods, employing thieves, acting against the law of the state, false weights and false measures, dealing in counterfeit goods. Among these five [whatever lapse arose …].
idiomaticTranslation: The third vow: I abstain, at a layperson's measure, from taking what has not been given. My firm rule is never to take — un-given — any prized possession, living or lifeless, nor any contraband whose theft the king would punish; yet I keep an honest leeway for trifles taken in the ordinary course of life: a wisp of grass, a stick of firewood, a thing dropped on the road, and the unavoidable minimum in matters of toll. Now I search out the five ways this vow may have been stained: handling stolen goods, setting thieves to work, breaking the lawful order of the state, cheating with false weights and measures, and trading in counterfeit wares. Whichever of these five I have committed —

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse turns the confession from speech (vow 2) to property, and its architecture is the niyama/jayaṇā hinge made unusually visible. The first half draws the *firm rule* tightly around what truly belongs to another: the *piyāruṁ* (precious) and the *rājanigraha-kārīuṁ* (contraband), specified across both *sacitta* and *acitta* — living chattel and lifeless goods alike. The second half then exhales into *jayaṇā*: *sūkṣma* trifles — *tṛṇā* (grass), *iṁdhaṇā* (firewood), *pathipatita* (roadside-fallen) things — and the toll-matter *dāṇacorī*, all hedged by *vavahāraniyoge*, 'only within ordinary good-faith dealing.' The point is precise: the householder cannot live without picking up a blade of grass or a fallen twig un-asked, so the vow does not pretend otherwise; it forbids the valuable and the unlawful, and merely cautions the trivial. The five Prākrit atichāras that follow are deliberately *not* outright theft — that would break the vow, not merely stain it. *Tenāhaḍe* and *takkarappaoge* implicate one in another's theft (receiving, commissioning); *viruddharajjāikamme* extends stealing to the state (smuggling, breach of lawful order); and the last two — *kūḍatullakūḍamāṇe* and *tappaḍiruvagavavahāre* — locate theft squarely in the marketplace: rigged scales and counterfeit goods. The shopkeeper who never 'steals' yet shorts every weight is the verse's quiet target. The closing *māṁhe* is grammatically incomplete on purpose, waiting for the day/night confession-refrain the reciter supplies from the template.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The five atichāras here match the canonical asteya-aṇuvrata list of Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.27 (stena-āhṛta, taskara-prayoga, viruddha-rājyātikrama, kūṭa-tulā-kūṭa-māna, pratirūpaka-vyavahāra), confirming that this vernacular verse transmits the orthodox śrāvakācāra intact. Doctrinally the verse teaches that *asteya* is violated not only by the hand that takes but by the consenting mind and the dishonest transaction — Jain ethics counts instigation, receipt, and commercial fraud as forms of theft because all arise from *lobha* (greed) and all appropriate what is *adatta*. The sacitta/acitta pairing reflects the Jain conviction that even living beings can be wrongful 'property,' tying non-stealing back to ahiṁsā. Crucially, the atichāra category itself embodies graded renunciation: a layperson who falls into false weights has *weakened* the vow, not destroyed it, and so confesses rather than re-takes it.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a modern reciter this is the vow of business ethics and everyday honesty: no rigged scales or counterfeit goods translates directly into accurate billing, honest measurement, no trading in stolen or pirated items, no tax/duty fraud, and no quietly profiting from another's wrongdoing. The verse also models a realistic conscience — it does not demand scruple over a borrowed pen or a fallen leaf, but draws the line firmly at anything of value taken un-given.

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra, Chapter 7 (vows and their atichāras), sūtra 7.27
sources.consultedFor: Canonical Sanskrit list of the five asteya atichāras to confirm the verse's Prākrit terms (stenāhṛta, taskaraprayoga, viruddharājyātikrama, kūṭatulākūṭamāna, pratirūpakavyavahāra).

sources.title: Anuvrata / Śrāvaka (Jain lay vows)
sources.consultedFor: Framework of the five aṇuvratas and the third (non-stealing) vow's place among the householder's twelve vows.

sources.title: Jainism for Householders — the five vows and their atichāras
sources.consultedFor: Glosses of the third-vow atichāras and the sacitta/acitta and trifling-items (grass, fuel, roadside-fallen) distinctions.

sources.title: Jainqq digital library — śrāvaka vrata and atichāra material
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the Prākrit atichāra-term meanings against śrāvakācāra commentary.

notes: The five Prākrit atichāra-terms map cleanly onto Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.27 and are confidently glossed. 'rājanigraha-kārīuṁ' is read as 'goods whose taking incurs royal penalty' (contraband); a minority reading takes it adjectivally with the act of taking ('taking that provokes state seizure') — sense is materially the same. 'dāṇacorī-jayaṇā' is read as the conscientious minimum permitted in unavoidable toll/customs matters (dāṇa = toll), not as license to evade duty; the book's spelling and the elliptical close 'māṁhe' (completed by the day/night refrain per the recurring template) are preserved verbatim. Vernacular Gujarati participles (kārīuṁ, aṇadīdhuṁ) are glossed from standard Gujarati; no source independently fixes their exact morpho-syntax here, so the syntactic bracketing of the first sentence carries modest uncertainty.
